content:
# Mark Chapter 4 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 33r–33v (PDF pages 65–66) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_004_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_004_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ And again he-began to-teach beside the sea. And is-gathered to him a-very-large crowd, so-that he, having-embarked into a-boat, sat in the sea; and all the crowd was toward the sea upon the land.

² And he-was-teaching them in parables many-[things], and was-saying to-them in his teaching:

³ "Listen! Behold, the sower went-out to-sow.

⁴ And it-came-to-pass in the sowing, some fell beside the path, and the birds came and devoured it.

⁵ And other fell upon the rocky-[ground], where it-had not much earth, and immediately it-sprang-up on-account-of not having depth of-earth;

⁶ and when the sun rose, it-was-scorched, and on-account-of not having root, it-withered.

⁷ And other fell into the thorns, and the thorns went-up and choked it, and fruit it-gave not.

⁸ And other fell into the good earth and was-giving fruit, going-up and growing, and was-bearing — one thirty, and one sixty, and one a-hundred."

⁹ And he-was-saying, "The-one having ears to-hear, let-him-hear."

¹⁰ And when he-was alone, the-ones around him with the twelve were-asking him [about] the parables.

¹¹ And he-was-saying to-them, "To-you the mystery of-the kingdom of God has-been-given; but to-those outside, in parables all-things come-into-being,

¹² in-order-that 'seeing they-may-see and not perceive, and hearing they-may-hear and not understand, lest they-should-turn and it-be-forgiven them.'"

¹³ And he-says to-them, "Do-you not know this parable? And how will-you-know all the parables?

¹⁴ The sower sows the word.

¹⁵ And these are the-ones beside the path where the word is-sown, and whenever they-hear, immediately the Satan comes and takes-away the word, the-[one] having-been-sown into them.

¹⁶ And these are likewise the-ones upon the rocky-[places] being-sown, who, whenever they-hear the word, immediately with joy receive it,

¹⁷ and they-have not root in themselves but are temporary; then [when] tribulation or persecution comes-into-being on-account-of the word, immediately they-are-scandalized.

¹⁸ And others are the-ones into the thorns being-sown — these are the-ones having-heard the word,

¹⁹ and the cares of-the age and the deceitfulness of-wealth and the desires concerning the remaining-[things] entering choke the word, and it-becomes fruitless.

²⁰ And those are the-ones upon the good earth having-been-sown, who hear the word and receive-[it] and bear-fruit — one thirty, and one sixty, and one a-hundred."

²¹ And he-was-saying to-them, "Does the lamp come in-order-that under the basket it-should-be-placed or under the bed? [Is it] not in-order-that upon the lampstand it-should-be-placed?

²² For there-is not [anything] hidden except in-order-that it-should-be-manifested, nor did-[anything]-become secret but that it-should-come into [the] open.

²³ If anyone has ears to-hear, let-him-hear."

²⁴ And he-was-saying to-them, "Watch-out what you-hear. With what measure you-measure, it-will-be-measured to-you, and [more] will-be-added to-you.

²⁵ For whoever has, it-will-be-given to-him; and whoever has not, even what he-has will-be-taken from him."

²⁶ And he-was-saying, "Thus is the kingdom of God — as a-man should-cast the seed upon the earth,

²⁷ and should-sleep and should-rise night and day, and the seed sprouts and grows — how, he himself does not know.

²⁸ By-itself the earth bears-fruit: first a-blade, then an-ear, then full grain in the ear.

²⁹ And whenever the fruit permits, immediately he-sends-forth the sickle, because the harvest has-stood-near."

³⁰ And he-was-saying, "How shall-we-liken the kingdom of God, or in what parable shall-we-place it?

³¹ As a-grain of-mustard, which whenever it-is-sown upon the earth, is smaller-than all the seeds upon the earth,

³² and whenever it-is-sown, it-goes-up and becomes greater-than all the garden-plants and makes great branches, so-that under its shadow the birds of-the heaven are-able to-dwell."

³³ And with many such parables he-was-speaking to-them the word, as they-were-able to-hear.

³⁴ And without a-parable he-was not speaking to-them; but privately to-his own disciples he-was-explaining all-[things].

³⁵ And he-says to-them on that day, evening having-come, "Let-us-pass-over to the other-side."

³⁶ And leaving the crowd, they-take him along, as he-was, in the boat; and other boats were with him.

³⁷ And comes-into-being a-great storm of-wind, and the waves were-casting into the boat, so-that already the boat was-being-filled.

³⁸ And he himself was in the stern, upon the cushion, sleeping. And they-raise him and say to-him, "Teacher, does-it not concern you that we-are-perishing?"

³⁹ And having-been-awakened, he-rebuked the wind and said to-the sea, "Be-silent! Be-still!" And the wind ceased, and there-came-into-being a-great calm.

⁴⁰ And he-said to-them, "Why are-you cowardly? Do-you not yet have faith?"

⁴¹ And they-feared [with] a-great fear and were-saying to one-another, "Who then is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?"

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **4:12 — "in order that" (ΙΝΑ):** The conjunction ΙΝΑ introduces a purpose clause, indicating that the parables are spoken so that outsiders will not understand. This is a difficult saying. I preserve the purposive force of the Greek.

2. **4:12 — Isaiah 6:9-10 allusion:** Mark's quotation is abbreviated and altered from the LXX. The final clause "lest they should turn and it be forgiven them" (ΜΗΠΟΤΕ ΕΠΙΣΤΡΕΨΩΣΙΝ ΚΑΙ ΑΦΕΘΗ ΑΥΤΟΙΣ) is unique to Mark's version.

3. **4:28 — "by itself" (ΑΥΤΟΜΑΤΗ):** The word αὐτομάτη ("of itself, spontaneously") emphasizes the self-generating power of the seed — the earth produces fruit without human agency.

4. **4:29 — "sends forth the sickle":** An allusion to Joel 3:13 (LXX 4:13). The verb ἀποστέλλει ("sends forth") connects to the apostolic commission.

5. **4:38 — "upon the cushion":** The definite article (ΤΟ ΠΡΟΣΚΕΦΑΛΑΙΟΝ) — "the cushion" — is a small, vivid detail unique to Mark, suggesting an eyewitness memory.

6. **4:40 — "cowardly" (ΔΕΙΛΟΙ):** Stronger than "afraid" — the word connotes cowardice, a failure of nerve. Mark's Jesus is direct in his challenge to the disciples.

— EMET, AI Translator
